title: Saj Hoffman-Hussain -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Saj Hoffman-Hussain -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: I'm good.
How are you, Andi?
Good, thanks.
Nice to meet you.
Thank you for scheduling some time.
Sorry I'm a little late.
I'm just, my brain is switching context very quickly.
Absolutely.
But where are you located?
I am actually based in Rochester, New York, so upstate New York.
Oh, great.
Awesome.
Well, thank you for, again, scheduling some time and for your patience.
I am excited to chat a little bit with you.
I can start by giving you a bit of feedback on myself and then the role, and especially how it fits within the context of GrowthX.
And then from there, we can talk about you and I'll try and leave five to ten minutes at the end for you to ask me any questions, but also like stop me or ask me anything as we're going to.
Sounds good.
Perfect.
So I lead customer operations at GrowthX.
I've been here for a little over two months now, which feels, sounds like a short amount of time, but we're less than a year old.
So we've been here for, you know, a significant period of GrowthX's lifetime.

And customer operations can mean a lot of things, but at GrowthX, what I'm really thinking about is the processes and kind of capacity planning pieces that go into delivering the content and contractual obligations for our clients.
So thinking about the way that we deliver content to our clients, there are kind of two aspects to this role.
The first is like a project management piece, which has a technical element to it.
GrowthX uses different, we leverage different AIs throughout our content production process.
And those are all built into a workflow that's on our own proprietary platform, which is internal today, may eventually be like a publicly available SaaS tool.
There's no timeline on that, but the, you know, this person is leveraged.

That internal platform, and so familiarity and ability to kind of express what you're looking to do with content, what might be working or not working within that platform to achieve your goals, and, you know, the ability to, like, ask for help from engineering in a way that engineering can kind of action on that feedback is really useful.
The platform that we're using today, we used to use AirOps, and then we've transitioned to our own platform, and it's six weeks old.
So it's still very new.
Obviously, that means it's a little bit buggy, but our engineering team, it's, like, kind of incredible how well it does work right now.
So, like, that's one piece.

And then, like, the other side of that project management is, you know, tracking all of the deliverables that we owe to a client, and thinking about, you know, letting a client know when something is.
We use Airtable for our kind of like just project management that a client can reference.
And then we use Slack as well, both internally and externally for client communications.
So comfort in the ability to, you know, provide updates and talk to clients about like where we are in their project plan is going to be really important as well.
You won't be the only person working on a client.
So we have, we basically work in pods today.
And so the technical managing editor kind of sits in the middle.

Again, as a project manager, we have content managers, which are essentially, they're essentially running the workflows on an individual basis because we're generating, you know, like 10-ish pieces of content a week for clients.
And a managing editor is managing roughly five clients at any given point of time.
So you'll be working.

With two to three content managers that are actually generating that content, you will be responsible for like editing, making sure that content is strong and is achieving the goals that the client set out to do, but also like helping the content manager troubleshoot work through kind of when the AI isn't generating exactly what we're looking for, working towards a solution there.

And then we also have directors who are the like strategic project leads for a given client, and directors manage two to three managing editors today, so like a couple of pods under a director, and so you'll be like really the main day-to-day point of contact for a client, and like the end person responsible for the output on a regular basis, but the director will help kind of shape tone, strategy, things like that, as, you know, as a contract progresses.
A lot of information.
Do you have any questions before we jump into you?
So, yeah, just sort of from my understanding, really, like I was speaking to the first person, and they mentioned that, you know, the project management is a big piece of this.

But also being aware, having, you know, worked in content management as well, being aware of that kind of workflow, and also having made myself deeply immersed in AI as well, being aware of that, the technical aspects, and as well as the solution aspects of that as well.
So, that's a great breakdown that you provided as well, and I have worked in similar parts as well.
So, it seems like that, in terms of kind of embedding, that would be ideal.
Perfect.
Well, let's talk about your experience.
I would love to know kind of your background, what you've been doing.
Most recently, and we'll go from there.
Sure.
So I have a background in journalism and content marketing.
So firstly, I worked as a journalist for BBC News for about eight years, producing different stories, strands across different lines of business and verticals.
And then from there, I transitioned into TV news production.
So I was a direct day-to-day orchestrator of how content was assembled and sourced and ultimately presented in a show format.
I was responsible for three of the new shows, the 3M news show, the noon news show, and the 4PM news show.
And so that required me to work with many different hats.
So in addition to being a TV producer, I was also responsible for the social media strategy during those shifts.
And then, when I fully transitioned into content marketing, I took on a very large project at a large national healthcare insurer.
So that was client-side, where I updated their web portals for their broker and their employee Medicare member constituents.
And that was with a goal of improving net promoter score and reducing the amount of phone complaints they'd been receiving.

And as part of that, you know, I worked through content audit, creating content calendars, you know, creating specific formats that worked for the uses that they had, speaking with internal stakeholders to make sure there was alignment there as well.
And then I moved into technical content management, where I worked at community brands, and I worked on their SaaS platform for associations.
And I worked with a team of other technical writers.
And
That I managed to produce product marketing content and technical content as well.

And then most recently worked at Publicist Sapient, where I was the head of content for thought leadership there, working on developing, you know, expert informed perspective content across multiple lines of business.
And in particular, in like regulated industries like healthcare and finance and, of course, AI as well.
I've been doing my own thing for the last year with simple content.
I've been kind of diving deep into RAG and AI and AI agents as well.
And I've been providing services focused around that.
So I have a good technical knowledge of different kinds of LLMs and also how to develop GPTs and the technical backgrounds around that as well.
And I've got background knowledge of some of the backend technical parts, as well as the creative storytelling and the content marketing.
Thank you.
We needed to like really succeed in this role.
In addition, a lot of my roles over the last couple of years have been very client-facing.
And so I'm used to presenting to clients on a regular basis, multiple clients.
And my last place that I worked at was an agency called True Collective.
And their big whale client was Wikipedia.
And I always saw the social media strategy for them as well in the content calendars.
So I'm used to interfacing at various different levels, whether it's a small-scale client or whether it's an enterprise client, I can end up my approach accordingly.
That was an amazing overview and like really good through line.
So thank you.
Curious to understand kind of why you're looking for something now.
I think like I'm just on your LinkedIn.
Like your work seems super interesting and fun.
So yeah, what are you looking for in like to be employed versus self-employed?
Yeah, I enjoy the work that I do.
I've been looking for more of a kind of like direct face, like kind of interaction with team environment.
So as a solo consultant, it can get quite, it can get quite isolating, you know, even though you're embedded with teams and things like that.
So I've been looking to kind of grow with a team and really there's a great opportunity to use the stuff that I've learned and then also build within a company and build a track record there as well.
So that's what I'm looking for really just, and in a way that you guys are, you know, really high growth, you know, you guys only have been around, what, 11 months as well.
And already the growth is insane, you know, and being there at the very start of something that I feel like would be, you know, potentially huge is very exciting to me.
So that's some of the main motivations for why.
Awesome.
Well, I...
I am not your, like, technical interviewer.
I am not, like, deep into the workflows or anything.
I'm kind of thinking more about, like, how they function as part of the whole.
But I'm curious in terms of, like, the work that you've been doing most recently and, like, diving into how to develop, like, those GPTs and thinking about the technical pieces.
I'm curious what has, like, surprised you or what are you most interested in in what's happening in that world right now?
So I've been learning a lot about how to kind of manage hallucinations, which, you know, is a, you know, key talking point in AI.
So, and so as part of that, there was a GPT that I created for a legal services client called CaseMind.
I'm happy to share the link to that for you as well as you can have a look.
And basically set up the framework for doing different tasks.
So not replacing the work of, you know, a paralegal, but like trying to help them kind of organize their caseload as well.
And then look at the stuff that they have already.
Like, so for example, I taught myself how to integrate an API with my GPT so that it could like speak to their CRM, their customer relationship management software as well.
I'm not an engineer, but, you know, I am good at like picking things up and learning things.
So, and I'm a forever learner.
So that's why I, you know, I like learning things like that.
And that was a really good experience because it taught me where my limitations were and how to kind of address those, you know, and where I was like testing my assumptions.

Like, for example, I thought initially it might be just a case of really creating some defined prompts, you know, whereas I realized there was a lot more in the back end that needs to be like refined.
So from an end to end perspective, I got a good appreciation.
appreciation.

Doing it myself, like, you know, from the technical part, engineering technical part to, you know, again, the roadmap, the operational roadmap and making sure that I stayed on track and, you know, over communicated and kept the client in control.

So I have a good idea of the big picture of it, you know, so that I'm able to appreciate when other folks are, you know, have their priorities and, like, collaborate with them, like, in a cross-collaborative way, you know, and just see beyond my, you know, day-to-day tasks, which I think will be very beneficial in this role, being able to, like, get in there and, you know, and work well with others as well, so.
Yeah, amazing.

I think, like, I was just thinking about one of the problems that we're actually dealing with with one of our clients right now is we're running article refreshes, and the workflow is, like, efficient, so we can do, like, 100 article refreshes in a day, but we found, like, the first run of 100.

100 was a little bit funky, like the content was good, but some of the formatting was a little bit off, and like there were some like weird grammatical errors, and we had like, when we had tested, we didn't just run 100, so when we tested in the first few, we didn't really see that, but we were anxious to start, move and get testing, and just like start to see the results, because this client has like 4,000 pages, and we were like, let's just start changing these, because they're kind of bold and kind of crappy anyways.

But they came back, and suddenly they were like, meh, these are like, yes, they're refreshed, but like we want these to be good, and running the content through the workflow again was just like a 100% guaranteed like hallucination all over the place, like it couldn't just deal with like, hey, fix these like five things that we see across this content, so super top of mind for me as well.
Can I ask a question around that, actually?
Yeah, sure.
So, like, you know, when you were, like, creating, like, the GPT or whatever, did you think about the template that was being produced on it, like, or formats and stuff?
Yeah, so we have, we essentially, like, when we're producing content, run, like, three workflows through the process.
So the first is, like, sort of, like, keyword or ideas generator, thinking about, like, just this is the, like, breadth of content that we want to focus on, these are the topics, things like that.

Then we have, like, outline or brief generator, and so we will put together the article brief, and then the actual article generation workflow separately is, includes the template for, like, this is the format, like, you know, these are the headers, these are the subheaders, this is where, like,
Andi-Hussain, not sure like to what extent we use all of those in the workflow refresh run, but I know that we do like generate the template.

One of the things that was doing was like, it wasn't rendering tables, it was just showing like, it would just build columns, and then they wouldn't wouldn't be a line, and then there wasn't any like table formatting.
Yeah.
That's, for me, that's clues, right?
You know?
Yeah.
That you can use to like kind of refine the workflow.
like, let's maybe say that, hey, you put in a check at the end, QA, that, you know, double check spelling, know, double check format, you know, are there any broken tables?
Don't produce this until the tables are fixed, you know, type thing.
And then that kind of, like, creates a little, like, virtuous loop where you know that whatever you're going to get at the end, at the very least, is going to be formatted correctly.
And I had a similar situation because I do a podcast with my friend Abdul, and we do our own content, and I created the cover image using the Ghibli style, right?
You know, he sent me his image, and I had my photo image as well, and we created this cool little image of us, you know, just chatting, you know, like that.
And I said, I wanted to use it for different topics.
And so when I, I have chat GPT+, so I was like, you know, hey, listen, I want to put this in different backgrounds.
And I really had to, like, identify the background structure of it, you know.
I'd say, this is the code I want you to use.
Don't change the faces, because it kept changing, like, it kept changing at different occurrences of us.
So, like, different cartoons.
I was like, no, no, I like the, I like the first one.
Right?
Use the code from the first one.
Show me the first list, list out the code, the VBA.
And then use the code from the first one.
So that I know you're going to use the same image in the background.
It's like little diagnostic ways that you can like kind of encourage it to not hallucinate as well.
And I also, as a client, I recently wrote, it's another SaaS client called Data Robot.
I wrote a piece on hallucinations and how you can use those as diagnostic indicators as well.
So if you look for AI hallucination Data Robot, it's a bylined article.
The subject matter expert is May Masood, you know, but I did all the preparation and research for that as well.
And I also write on AI for my sub stack as well.
So.
Amazing.
Well, let's talk a little bit about your client management and like kind of project management style.
And then we'll open it up for questions for you.
But like how many clients are you working with on an ongoing basis?
And how do you think about.
What's prioritization there?
Well, currently I've got two or three, I mentioned before that, two or three clients that I'm working with on a semi-casual basis right now.

And so the way that I prioritize that is, you know, similar systems like Airtable to like prioritize in terms of like what our immediate needs, what are, you know, what are longer term priorities, you know, what are midterm priorities and how do we scope those out, right?
So let's say you're using a PM system, like, you know, you can do that with points, for example, numbers.

You can do that with high, low, medium, you know, you can do that with hours dedicated, you can do that with, you know, you can just create your own little system that's from one to 10, you know, so that you can create the weightings out, maybe use the Gantt chart to plan it all out, you know, as well.
And the key thing to all of this is to really be aligned with what your, you know, your client wants, right, and what they expect, so that you're on the same page.
Then when you create your project plan or your roadmap, it's not something that just comes out, you know, from your head and say...
I think it's based on data, it's based on, you know, the insights that you've gathered, rather than, you know, this is my gut that I feel like would be a good plan.

I did that for a particular project at TerraKey, where I created an own website venture for a top national mortgage provider called MoneyTips.com, which was basically meant to be a finance advice site that was agnostically oriented for Gen Z and millennial first-time homebuyers.

And spoke with the client, interface with them, made sure I understood what their priorities were in terms of the product line that they were selling, you know, and how we could reference that in an agnostic way on the site, and create interesting, resonant topics that folks were, you know, were excited to consume, and then take the next step towards, like, you know, filling in that mortgage form application, and getting that call and that qualified lead from the top mortgage provider as well.
So.

So just being really organized and being aware of what, you know, what your to-dos are, you know, and how those are scoped out and being able to be nimble as well and being able to put flexibility within the system because sometimes priorities change, right?
Like, for example, there was a personal loan calculator that we were asked to do.
And then at the last minute, they mentioned that actually they wanted that as a greater interactive calculator, like overall for mortgage.
And so we had to widen the scope out and then decide what had to go in terms of our current priorities.
Similarly, you know, when they asked us to scale up from 75 articles a month to 200 articles a month, we had to consider, you know, resourcing and how we best resource that as well.
Part of the way that I addressed that, along with the PM at the time, was to look at Upwork and contact their professional arm.
We have used those.
Vendors and what have you.

similar, I published a Sapient for articles that had specific niche knowledge that my team didn't have, you know, looked outside, you know, like there was a third party vendor called Crowd Contact, which is based in London.
And I worked with those guys, they were really good, you know, creating those really sourced and bylined and experienced articles as well.
And just being able to over communicate and make sure people are aware.
And also just so you can like generate that kind of awareness of what's important and generate buy in around them.
Awesome.
Cool.
We have five minutes left.
So I want to make sure that there's time for you to ask me questions.
So what can I answer for you?
So you've been, you've been there two months.
I mean, you know, sounds like you dove in.
Where were you before this?
So I was at another series.
A startup that didn't raise at Series B, so I was there through the fall of last year, and then took the winter off, and found GrowthX.
Really, they found me, but, you know, what I was looking for, and kind of like my expertise is, like, creating process from chaos.
Chaos.
Not that this was pure chaos, but, you know, everybody was just so focused on deliverables that we didn't have, like, unified systems yet.
So that's been really what I've been working on so far.
Excellent.
Yeah, I mean, part of my remit at Teraki, I actually overhauled their topic opportunity reporting.

So, like, they operated in a pod-like structure as well, and every single pod served different clients, and they had different approaches, which, you know, again, from a selling perspective, you know, when...
If you're trying to sell that as a client or a client lead or an account executive, it's hard to say, you know, you're to get the same services as, you know, throughout the org as a vendor.
So without having that clear governance and, you know, those who's responsible, the races and things like that.
So I worked with the different leads, technical leads to create races and content governance and workflows and operations.
And they were able to package that within their own proprietary system and be able to use that as a selling point when they were going out for outreach as well.
So I'm also very process oriented from that perspective and able to kind of like break those workflow parts down as well.
Also able to zoom out and look at the bigger picture and look at the longer term goals as well.
But that's also my jam as well.
That's great.
So how have you found it the two months that you've been, what's, what's, what's been your favorite part?
Um, I think like people are very.

passionate about, they want to, like, this is a team of people who's, like, willing to work hard and wants to do a good job, and that is, like, it's always nice to know that you're all, like, willing to put in the work, and with the level of growth, like, people are excited about the prospect, and there's some huge talent on this team.
Like, on the engineering side, just incredible that, you know, we've had a platform live for six weeks, and it's, you know, generating hundreds of pieces of content a week for us.
And, yeah, I think, like, that has been incredible.
I, like, didn't work, I hadn't worked in an AI company before, like, really been very AI-focused, so learning a lot on that front, too.
But, like, the possibilities are really exciting here.
Awesome, awesome.
And in terms of, like, you know, kind of the priorities that grows to X, coming up for the next.

Six months, is it just basically more of the same in terms of like acquiring clients or are you guys betting in on the existing clients that you have and trying to kind of add up the offerings for them?

Yeah, so right now we're actually refining our offering a little bit because in acquiring clients and honestly our CEO has like been the person that has signed most of our deals so far because he is like, he just has an incredible network and is a really like compelling salesman.
And so he has, and he's very creative.
So we have a lot of like variations in what our partnerships look like today.

We're trying to unify that so that we can scale the deliverable that we're best at before we start expanding the work streams that we're selling because we are, we definitely offer like content blog generation and content refreshes.
That's what we do super well.
We sometimes get into scope creep of clients being like, you do this so well.
Can we do this?
Can we do this?
Can we do that?
And so we're figuring out how to unify that.
And then also, we are approaching one year, figuring out what the life cycle of a client looks like, because we've had a lot of growth, but not every client, I mean, we also have churn.
And so, like, understanding, what can we expect from the life cycle of a client?
And I'm so sorry, but we are at time.
And I have a client call right now.
But it was really amazing to talk to you.
And I hope that I will chat with you again.
But I think, like, you'll probably end up chatting with Panzer.
And you will probably enjoy that conversation, because he's much more technical than I am.
So looking forward to chatting with you again.
Lovely to chat with you, Andi.
Thanks, Saj.
Bye.
The recording.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview for Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=169.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role combines project management, AI workflow expertise, and client-facing responsibilities](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=184.0)
  - [Saj has extensive experience in journalism, content marketing, and AI/GPT development](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=545.0)
  - [GrowthX is a rapidly growing startup (11 months old) with a proprietary AI content platform](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=814.0)
  - [Position manages \~5 clients, overseeing content production of \~10 pieces/week per client](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=412.0)

## Topics

### Role Overview

  - [Part of Customer Operations team, led by Andi (2 months at GrowthX)](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=222.0)
  - [Focuses on content delivery processes and capacity planning](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=240.0)
  - [Uses proprietary internal AI platform (6 weeks old, may become SaaS tool)](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=283.0)
  - [Manages \~5 clients, working with 2-3 content managers](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=412.0)
  - [Reports to a director who oversees 2-3 managing editors](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=442.0)
  - [Key responsibilities:](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=345.0)
      - [Project management (technical + client-facing)](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=269.0)
      - [Content editing and quality assurance](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
      - [AI workflow troubleshooting](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
      - [Client communication and updates](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=373.0)

### GrowthX Structure and Tools

  - [Works in "pods" with content managers and directors](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=388.0)
  - [Uses Airtable for project management](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [Slack for internal and external communication](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=367.0)
  - [Proprietary AI platform for content generation](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=283.0)
  - [Transitioning from AirOps to in-house platform](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=327.0)

### Saj's Background

  - [8 years at BBC News (journalism)](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=551.0)
  - [TV news production (orchestrating 3 daily shows)](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=567.0)
  - [Content marketing for healthcare insurer (web portal updates)](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=601.0)
  - [Technical content management for SaaS platform](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=645.0)
  - [Head of content for thought leadership at Publicis Sapient](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=666.0)
  - [Recent focus on RAG, AI agents, and GPT development](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=687.0)
  - [Created CaseMind GPT for legal services client](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=890.0)
  - [Experienced in client-facing roles and enterprise clients (e.g., Wikipedia)](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=732.0)

### AI and Content Challenges

  - [GrowthX facing issues with article refreshes (formatting, grammar)](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=1052.0)
  - [Saj's experience with managing AI hallucinations](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=870.0)
  - [Discussion on refining AI workflows and implementing QA checks](https://fathom.video/share/2rveRgwbUC4qYhpSy8ifjrqAdvJgzCy8?tab=summary&timestamp=1095.0)

### Client/Project Management Approach
